properties.notes: _Example: Given the following relationships, determine what the expression_ $S(T(t))$ _represents._
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description:
Find the approximate solutions of an equation graphically, using tables of values, or finding successive approximations, using technology where appropriate. [_Algebra I with Probability, 19_]

_Note: Include cases where $f(x)$ is linear, quadratic, exponential, or absolute value functions and $g(x)$ is constant or linear._
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Distinguish between linear and non-linear functions. [_Grade 8, 15a_]
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Write explicit and recursive formulas for arithmetic and geometric sequences and connect them to linear and exponential functions. [_Algebra I with Probability, 22_]
properties.notes: _Example: A sequence with constant growth will be a linear function, while a sequence with proportional growth will be an exponential function._
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Show that linear functions grow by equal differences over equal intervals, while exponential functions grow by equal factors over equal intervals.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Define linear functions to represent situations in which one quantity changes at a constant rate per unit interval relative to another.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Define exponential functions to represent situations in which a quantity grows or decays by a constant percent rate per unit interval relative to another. [_Algebra I with Probability, 24_]
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Graph linear and quadratic functions and show intercepts, maxima, and minima.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Graph piecewise-defined functions, including step functions and absolute value functions.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Graph exponential functions, showing intercepts and end behavior. [_Algebra I with Probability, 30_]
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Describe the rate of change and _y_-intercept in the context of a problem using a linear model of a real-world situation. [Grade 8, 20]
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Summarize categorical data for two categories in two-way frequency tables and represent using segmented bar graphs.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Interpret relative frequencies in the context of categorical data (including joint, marginal, and conditional relative frequencies).
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Identify possible associations and trends in categorical data. [_Algebra I with Probability, 35_]
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Aggregate data from several groups to find an overall association between two categorical variables.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Recognize and explore situations where the association between two categorical variables is reversed when a third variable is considered (Simpson's Paradox). [_Algebra I with Probability, 36_]
properties.notes: _Example: In a certain city, Hospital 1 has a higher fatality rate than Hospital 2. But when considering mildly-injured patients and severely-injured patients as separate groups, Hospital 1 has a lower fatality rate among both groups than Hospital 2, since Hospital 1 is a Level 1 Trauma Center. Thus, Hospital 1 receives most of the severely-injured patients who are less likely to survive overall but have a better chance of surviving in Hospital 1 than they would in Hospital 2._
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Choose and interpret units consistently in formulas.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Choose and interpret the scale and the origin in graphs and data displays.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Define appropriate quantities for the purpose of descriptive modeling.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Choose a level of accuracy appropriate to limitations of measurements when reporting quantities.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Find the coordinates of the vertices of a polygon determined by a set of lines, given their equations, by setting their function rules equal and solving, or by using their graphs.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Rearrange formulas to highlight a quantity of interest, using the same reasoning as in solving equations.
properties.notes: _Example: Rearrange the formula for the area of a trapezoid to highlight one of the bases._
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Verify that the graph of a linear equation in two variables is the set of all its solutions plotted in the coordinate plane, which forms a line.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Derive the equation of a circle of given center and radius using the Pythagorean Theorem.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use mathematical and statistical reasoning with quantitative data, both univariate data (set of values) and bivariate data (set of pairs of values) that suggest a linear association, in order to draw conclusions and assess risk.
properties.notes: _Example: Estimate the typical age at which a lung cancer patient is diagnosed, and estimate how the typical age differs depending on the number of cigarettes smoked per day._
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use technology to organize data, including very large data sets, into a useful and manageable structure.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Represent the distribution of univariate quantitative data with plots on the real number line, choosing a format (dot plot, histogram, or box plot) most appropriate to the data set, and represent the distribution of bivariate quantitative data with a scatter plot. **Extend from simple cases by hand to more complex cases involving large data sets using technology.**
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use statistics appropriate to the shape of the data distribution to compare and contrast two or more data sets, utilizing the mean and median for center and the interquartile range and standard deviation for variability.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Interpret differences in shape, center, and spread in the context of data sets, accounting for possible effects of extreme data points (outliers) on mean and standard deviation.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Represent data of two quantitative variables on a scatter plot, and describe how the variables are related.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Compute (using technology) and interpret the correlation coefficient of a linear relationship.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Distinguish between correlation and causation.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Evaluate possible solutions to real-life problems by developing linear models of contextual situations and using them to predict unknown values.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Identify the shapes of two-dimensional cross-sections of three-dimensional objects, and identify three-dimensional objects generated by rotations of two-dimensional objects.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Model and solve problems using surface area and volume of solids, including composite solids and solids with portions removed.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Given the coordinates of the vertices of a polygon, compute its perimeter and area using a variety of methods, including the distance formula and dynamic geometry software, and evaluate the accuracy of the results.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Derive and apply the relationships between the lengths, perimeters, areas, and volumes of similar figures in relation to their scale factor.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Derive and apply the formula for the length of an arc and the formula for the area of a sector.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Represent transformations and compositions of transformations in the plane (coordinate and otherwise) using tools such as tracing paper and geometry software.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Explore rotations, reflections, and translations using graph paper, tracing paper, and geometry software.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Develop definitions of rotation, reflection, and translation in terms of angles, circles, perpendicular lines, parallel lines, and line segments.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Define congruence of two figures in terms of rigid motions (a sequence of translations, rotations, and reflections); show that two figures are congruent by finding a sequence of rigid motions that maps one figure to the other.
properties.notes: _Example:_ $\Delta ABC$ _is congruent to_ $\Delta XYZ$ _since a reflection followed by a translation maps_ $\Delta ABC$ _onto_ $\Delta XYZ$.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Verify criteria for showing triangles are congruent using a sequence of rigid motions that map one triangle to another.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Verify experimentally the properties of dilations given by a center and a scale factor.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Given two figures, determine whether they are similar by identifying a similarity transformation (sequence of rigid motions and dilations) that maps one figure to the other.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Verify criteria for showing triangles are similar using a similarity transformation (sequence of rigid motions and dilations) that maps one triangle to another.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Find patterns and relationships in figures including lines, triangles, quadrilaterals, and circles, using technology and other tools.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Develop and use precise definitions of figures such as angle, circle, perpendicular lines, parallel lines, and line segment, based on the undefined notions of point, line, distance along a line, and distance around a circular arc.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Justify whether conjectures are true or false in order to prove theorems and then apply those theorems in solving problems, communicating proofs in a variety of ways, including flow chart, two-column, and paragraph formats.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use coordinates to prove simple geometric theorems algebraically.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Prove the slope criteria for parallel and perpendicular lines and use them to solve geometric problems.
properties.notes: _Example: Find the equation of a line parallel or perpendicular to a given line that passes through a given point._
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use congruence and similarity criteria for triangles to solve problems in real-world contexts.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Discover and apply relationships in similar right triangles.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use geometric shapes, their measures, and their properties to model objects and use those models to solve problems.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Investigate and apply relationships among inscribed angles, radii, and chords, including but not limited to: the relationship between central, inscribed, and circumscribed angles; inscribed angles on a diameter are right angles; the radius of a circle is perpendicular to the tangent where the radius intersects the circle.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use the mathematical modeling cycle involving geometric methods to solve design problems.
properties.notes: _Examples: Design an object or structure to satisfy physical constraints or minimize cost; work with typographic grid systems based on ratios; apply concepts of density based on area and volume._
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Interpret linear, quadratic, and exponential expressions in terms of a context by viewing one or more of their parts as a single entity.
properties.notes: _Example: Interpret the accrued amount of investment_ $P(1 + r)^t$, _where_ $P$ _is the principal and_ $r$ _is the interest rate, as the product of_ $P$ _and a factor depending on time_ $t$.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use the structure of an expression to identify ways to rewrite it.
properties.notes: _Example: See_ $x^4 - y^4$ as $(x^2)^2 - (y^2)^2$, _thus recognizing it as a difference of squares that can be factored as_ $(x^2 - y^2)(x^2 + y^2)$.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Choose and produce an equivalent form of an expression to reveal and explain properties of the quantity represented by the expression.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Add, subtract, and multiply polynomials, showing that polynomials form a system analogous to the integers, namely, they are closed under the operations of addition, subtraction, and multiplication.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Explain why extraneous solutions to an equation involving absolute values may arise and how to check to be sure that a candidate solution satisfies an equation.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Select an appropriate method to solve a quadratic equation in one variable.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Select an appropriate method to solve a system of two linear equations in two variables.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Create equations and inequalities in one variable and use them to solve problems in context, either exactly or approximately. **Extend from contexts arising from linear functions to those involving quadratic, exponential, and absolute value functions.**
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Create equations in two or more variables to represent relationships between quantities in context; graph equations on coordinate axes with labels and scales and use them to make predictions. **Limit to contexts arising from linear, quadratic, exponential, absolute value, and linear piecewise functions.**
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Represent constraints by equations and/or inequalities, and solve systems of equations and/or inequalities, interpreting solutions as viable or nonviable options in a modeling context. **Limit to contexts arising from linear, quadratic, exponential, absolute value, and linear piecewise functions.**
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Given a relation defined by an equation in two variables, identify the graph of the relation as the set of all its solutions plotted in the coordinate plane. _Note: The graph of a relation often forms a curve (which could be a line)._
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Define a function as a mapping from one set (called the domain) to another set (called the range) that assigns to each element of the domain exactly one element of the range.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Compare and contrast relations and functions represented by equations, graphs, or tables that show related values; determine whether a relation is a function. Explain that a function f is a special kind of relation defined by the equation $y = f(x)$.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Combine different types of standard functions to write, evaluate, and interpret functions in context. **Limit to linear, quadratic, exponential, and absolute value functions.**
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Solve systems consisting of linear and/or quadratic equations in two variables graphically, using technology where appropriate.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Explain why the _x_-coordinates of the points where the graphs of the equations $y = f(x)$ and $y = g(x)$ intersect are the solutions of the equation $f(x) = g(x)$.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Graph the solutions to a linear inequality in two variables as a half-plane (excluding the boundary in the case of a strict inequality), and graph the solution set to a system of linear inequalities in two variables as the intersection of the corresponding half-planes, using technology where appropriate.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Compare properties of two functions, each represented in a different way (algebraically, graphically, numerically in tables, or by verbal descriptions). **Extend from linear to quadratic, exponential, absolute value, and general piecewise.**
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Define sequences as functions, including recursive definitions, whose domain is a subset of the integers.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Identify the effect on the graph of replacing $f(x)$ by $f(x) + k$, $k \cdot f(x)$, $f(k \cdot x)$ and $f(x + k)$ for specific values of $k$ (both positive and negative); find the value of $k$ given the graphs. Experiment with cases and explain the effects on the graph, using technology as appropriate. **Limit to linear, quadratic, exponential, absolute value, and linear piecewise functions.**
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Distinguish between situations that can be modeled with linear functions and those that can be modeled with exponential functions.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Construct linear and exponential functions, including arithmetic and geometric sequences, given a graph, a description of a relationship, or two input-output pairs (include reading these from a table).
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use graphs and tables to show that a quantity increasing exponentially eventually exceeds a quantity increasing linearly or quadratically.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Interpret the parameters of functions in terms of a context. **Extend from linear functions, written in the form $mx + b$, to exponential functions, written in the form $ab^x$.**
properties.notes: _Example: If the function_ $V(t) = 19885(0.75)^t$ _describes the value of a car after it has been owned for_ $t$ _years, 19885 represents the purchase price of the car when_ $t = 0$, _and 0.75 represents the annual rate at which its value decreases._
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: For a function that models a relationship between two quantities, interpret key features of graphs and tables in terms of the quantities, and sketch graphs showing key features given a verbal description of the relationship. _Note: Key features include: intercepts; intervals where the function is increasing, decreasing, positive, or negative; maximums and minimums; symmetries; and end behavior._ **Extend from relationships that can be represented by linear functions to quadratic, exponential, absolute value, and linear piecewise functions.**
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Calculate and interpret the average rate of change of a function (presented symbolically or as a table) over a specified interval. Estimate the rate of change from a graph. **Limit to linear, quadratic, exponential, and absolute value functions.**
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Graph functions expressed symbolically and show key features of the graph, by hand in simple cases and using technology for more complicated cases.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use the mathematical modeling cycle to solve real-world problems involving linear, quadratic, exponential, absolute value, and linear piecewise functions.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use mathematical and statistical reasoning with bivariate categorical data in order to draw conclusions and assess risk.
properties.notes:
_Example: In a clinical trial comparing the effectiveness of flu shots A and B, 21 subjects in treatment group A avoided getting the flu while 29 contracted it. In group B, 12 avoided the flu while 13 contracted it. Discuss which flu shot appears to be more effective in reducing the chances of contracting the flu._

_Possible answer: Even though more people in group A avoided the flu than in group B, the proportion of people avoiding the flu in group B is greater than the proportion in group A, which suggests that treatment B may be more effective in lowering the risk of getting the flu._
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Design and carry out an investigation to determine whether there appears to be an association between two categorical variables, and write a persuasive argument based on the results of the investigation.
properties.notes: _Example: Investigate whether there appears to be an association between successfully completing a task in a given length of time and listening to music while attempting the task. Randomly assign some students to listen to music while attempting to complete the task and others to complete the task without listening to music. Discuss whether students should listen to music while studying, based on that analysis._
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Distinguish between quantitative and categorical data and between the techniques that may be used for analyzing data of these two types.
properties.notes: _Example: The color of cars is categorical and so is summarized by frequency and proportion for each color category, while the mileage on each car's odometer is quantitative and can be summarized by the mean._
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Analyze the possible association between two categorical variables.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Generate a two-way categorical table in order to find and evaluate solutions to real-world problems.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Describe events as subsets of a sample space (the set of outcomes) using characteristics (or categories) of the outcomes, or as unions, intersections, or complements of other events ("or," "and," "not").
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Explain whether two events, A and B, are independent, using two-way tables or tree diagrams.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Compute the conditional probability of event A given event B, using two-way tables or tree diagrams.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Recognize and describe the concepts of conditional probability and independence in everyday situations and explain them using everyday language.
properties.notes: _Example: Contrast the chance of having lung cancer if you are a smoker with the chance of being a smoker if you have lung cancer._
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Explain why the conditional probability of A given B is the fraction of B's outcomes that also belong to A, and interpret the answer in context.
properties.notes: _Example: the probability of drawing a king from a deck of cards, given that it is a face card, is_ $\frac{4/52}{12/52}$, _which is_ $\frac{1}{3}$.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Add, subtract, and multiply complex numbers using the commutative, associative, and distributive properties.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Find the additive and multiplicative inverses of square matrices, using technology as appropriate.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Explain the role of the determinant in determining if a square matrix has a multiplicative inverse.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Factor polynomials using common factoring techniques, and use the factored form of a polynomial to reveal the zeros of the function it defines.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Prove polynomial identities and use them to describe numerical relationships.
properties.notes: _Example: The polynomial identity_ $1 - x^n = (1 - x)(1 + x + x^2 + x^3 + ... + x^{n-1} + x^n)$ _can be used to find the sum of the first_ n _terms of a geometric sequence with common ratio_ x _by dividing both sides of the identity by_ $(1 - x)$.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Explain why extraneous solutions to an equation may arise and how to check to be sure that a candidate solution satisfies an equation. **Extend to radical equations.**
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: For exponential models, express as a logarithm the solution to $ab^{ct} = d$, where $a$, $c$, and $d$ are real numbers and the base $b$ is 2 or 10; evaluate the logarithm using technology to solve an exponential equation.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Create equations and inequalities in one variable and use them to solve problems. **Extend to equations arising from polynomial, trigonometric (sine and cosine), logarithmic, radical, and general piecewise functions.**
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Solve quadratic equations with real coefficients that have complex solutions.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Solve simple equations involving exponential, radical, logarithmic, and trigonometric functions using inverse functions.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Create equations in two or more variables to represent relationships between quantities; graph equations on coordinate axes with labels and scales and use them to make predictions. **Extend to polynomial, trigonometric (sine and cosine), logarithmic, reciprocal, radical, and general piecewise functions.**
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Explain why the _x_-coordinates of the points where the graphs of the equations $y = f(x)$ and $y = g(x)$ intersect are the solutions of the equation $f(x) = g(x)$.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Compare properties of two functions each represented in a different way (algebraically, graphically, numerically in tables, or by verbal descriptions). **Extend to polynomial, trigonometric (sine and cosine), logarithmic, radical, and general piecewise functions.**
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Identify the effect on the graph of replacing $f(x)$ by $f(x) + k$, $k \cdot f(x)$, $f(k \cdot x)$, and $f(x + k)$ for specific values of $k$ (both positive and negative); find the value of $k$ given the graphs. **Experiment with cases and illustrate an explanation of the effects on the graph using technology. Extend to polynomial, trigonometric (sine and cosine), logarithmic, reciprocal, radical, and general piecewise functions.**
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: For a function that models a relationship between two quantities, interpret key features of graphs and tables in terms of the quantities, and sketch graphs showing key features given a verbal description of the relationship. _Note: Key features include intercepts; intervals where the function is increasing, decreasing, positive, or negative; maximums and minimums; symmetries (including even and odd); end behavior; and periodicity._ **Extend to polynomial, trigonometric (sine and cosine), logarithmic, reciprocal, radical, and general piecewise functions.**
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Relate the domain of a function to its graph and, where applicable, to the quantitative relationship it describes. **Extend to polynomial, trigonometric (sine and cosine), logarithmic, reciprocal, radical, and general piecewise functions.**
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Calculate and interpret the average rate of change of a function (presented symbolically or as a table) over a specified interval. Estimate the rate of change from a graph. **Extend to polynomial, trigonometric (sine and cosine), logarithmic, reciprocal, radical, and general piecewise functions.**
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Graph functions expressed symbolically and show key features of the graph, by hand in simple cases and using technology for more complicated cases. **Extend to polynomial, trigonometric (sine and cosine), logarithmic, reciprocal, radical, and general piecewise functions.**
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Explain how the unit circle in the coordinate plane enables the extension of trigonometric functions to all real numbers, interpreted as radian measures of angles traversed counterclockwise around the unit circle, building on work with non-right triangle trigonometry.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use the mathematical modeling cycle to solve real-world problems involving polynomial, trigonometric (sine and cosine), logarithmic, radical, and general piecewise functions, from the simplification of the problem through the solving of the simplified problem, the interpretation of its solution, and the checking of the solution's feasibility.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use mathematical and statistical reasoning about normal distributions to draw conclusions and assess risk; limit to informal arguments.
properties.notes: _Example: If candidate A is leading candidate B by 2% in a poll which has a margin of error of less than 3%, should we be surprised if candidate B wins the election?_
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Design and carry out an experiment or survey to answer a question of interest, and write an informal persuasive argument based on the results.
properties.notes: _Example: Use the statistical problem-solving cycle to answer the question, "Is there an association between playing a musical instrument and doing well in mathematics?"_
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: From a normal distribution, use technology to find the mean and standard deviation and estimate population percentages by applying the empirical rule.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Describe the purposes of and differences among sample surveys, experiments, and observational studies; explain how randomization relates to each.
properties.notes: _Examples: random assignment in experiments, random selection in surveys and observational studies_
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Distinguish between a statistic and a parameter and use statistical processes to make inferences about population parameters based on statistics from random samples from that population.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Describe differences between randomly selecting samples and randomly assigning subjects to experimental treatment groups in terms of inferences drawn regarding a population versus regarding cause and effect.
properties.notes: _Example: Data from a group of plants randomly selected from a field allows inference regarding the rest of the plants in the field, while randomly assigning each plant to one of two treatments allows inference regarding differences in the effects of the two treatments. If the plants were both randomly selected and randomly assigned, we can infer that the difference in effects of the two treatments would also be observed when applied to the rest of the plants in the field._
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Explain the consequences, due to uncontrolled variables, of non-randomized assignment of subjects to groups in experiments.
properties.notes: _Example: Students are studying whether or not listening to music while completing mathematics homework improves their quiz scores. Rather than assigning students to either listen to music or not at random, they simply observe what the students do on their own and find that the music-listening group has a higher mean quiz score. Can they conclude that listening to music while studying is likely to raise the quiz scores of students who do not already listen to music? What other factors may have been responsible for the observed difference in mean quiz scores?_
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Evaluate where bias, including sampling, response, or nonresponse bias, may occur in surveys, and whether results are representative of the population of interest.
properties.notes: _Example: Selecting students eating lunch in the cafeteria to participate in a survey may not accurately represent the student body, as students who do not eat in the cafeteria may not be accounted for and may have different opinions, or students may not respond honestly to questions that may be embarrassing, such as how much time they spend on homework._
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Evaluate the effect of sample size on the expected variability in the sampling distribution of a sample statistic.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Produce a sampling distribution by repeatedly selecting samples of the same size from a given population or from a population simulated by bootstrapping (resampling with replacement from an observed sample). Do initial examples by hand, then use technology to generate a large number of samples.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use data from a randomized experiment to compare two treatments; limit to informal use of simulations to decide if an observed difference in the responses of the two treatment groups is unlikely to have occurred due to randomization alone, thus implying that the difference between the treatment groups is meaningful.
properties.notes: _Example: Fifteen students are randomly assigned to a treatment group that listens to music while completing mathematics homework and another 15 are assigned to a control group that does not, and their means on the next quiz are found to be different. To test whether the differences seem significant, all the scores from the two groups are placed on index cards and repeatedly shuffled into two new groups of 15 each, each time recording the difference in the means of the two groups. The differences in means of the treatment and control groups are then compared to the differences in means of the mixed groups to see how likely it is to occur._
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Define the radian measure of an angle as the constant of proportionality of the length of an arc it intercepts to the radius of the circle; in particular, it is the length of the arc intercepted on the unit circle.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Choose trigonometric functions (sine and cosine) to model periodic phenomena with specified amplitude, frequency, and midline.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Prove the Pythagorean identity $sin^2 (\theta) + cos^2 (\theta) = 1$ and use it to calculate trigonometric ratios.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Derive and apply the formula $A = \frac{1}{2} \cdot ab \cdot sin(C)$ for the area of a triangle by drawing an auxiliary line from a vertex perpendicular to the opposite side, extending the domain of sine to include right and obtuse angles.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Derive and apply the Law of Sines and the Law of Cosines to find unknown measurements in right and non-right triangles. **Extend the domain of sine and cosine to include right and obtuse angles.**
properties.notes: _Examples: surveying problems, resultant forces_
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Explain the relationship between annual percentage yield (APY) and annual percentage rate (APR) as values for $r$ in the formulas $A=P(1+r)^t$ and $A=Pe^{rt}$.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Research and evaluate various mortgage products available to consumers.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Compare monthly mortgage payments for different terms, interest rates, and down payments.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Analyze the financial consequence of buying a home (mortgage payments vs. potentially increasing resale value) versus investing the money saved when renting, assuming that renting is the less expensive option.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Determine the level of precision and the appropriate tools for taking the measurements in constructing a two-dimensional visual representation of a three-dimensional object or structure.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Create an elevation drawing to represent a given solid structure, using technology where appropriate.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Determine which measurements cannot be taken directly and must be calculated based on other measurements when constructing a two-dimensional visual representation of a three-dimensional object or structure.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Determine an appropriate means to visually represent an object or structure, such as drawings on paper or graphics on computer screens.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Describe the features of a three-dimensional Cartesian coordinate system and use them to graph points.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Graph a point in space as the vertex of a right prism drawn in the appropriate octant with edges along the _x, y_, and _z_ axes.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Find the distance between two objects in space given the coordinates of each.
properties.notes: _Examples: Determine whether two aircraft are flying far enough apart to be safe; find how long a zipline cable would need to be to connect two platforms at different heights on two trees._
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Find the midpoint between two objects in space given the coordinates of each.
properties.notes: _Example: If two asteroids in space are traveling toward each other at the same speed, find where they will collide._
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Create a scatter plot with a sufficient number of data points to predict a pattern.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Describe the overall relationship between two quantitative variables (increase, decrease, linearity, concavity, extrema, inflection) or pattern of change.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Make a prediction based upon patterns.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Estimate the probability of each value for a random variable based on empirical observations or simulations, using technology.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Represent a probability distribution by a relative frequency histogram and/or a cumulative relative frequency graph.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Find the mean, standard deviation, median, and interquartile range of a probability distribution and make long-term predictions about future possibilities. Determine which measures are most appropriate based upon the shape of the distribution.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use the binomial theorem to construct the sampling distribution for the number of successes in a binary event or the number of positive responses to a yes/no question in a random sample.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use the normal approximation of a proportion from a random event or sample when conditions are met.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use the central limit theorem to construct a normal sampling distribution for the sample mean when conditions are met.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Find the long-term probability of a given range of outcomes from a random event or random sample.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use a point estimator and margin of error to construct a confidence interval for a proportion or mean.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Interpret a confidence interval in context and use it to make strategic decisions.
properties.notes: _Example: short-term and long-term budget projections for a business_
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Perform a significance test for null and alternative hypotheses.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Interpret the significance level of a test in the context of error probabilities, and use the results to make strategic decisions.
properties.notes: _Example: How do you reduce the rate of human error on the floor of a manufacturing plant?_
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Determine whether statements are equivalent and construct equivalent statements.
properties.notes: _Example: Show that the contrapositive of a statement is its logical equivalent._
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Determine whether a logical argument is a tautology or a contradiction.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use various counting models (including tree diagrams and lists) to identify the distinguishing factors of a context in which the Fundamental Counting Principle can be applied.
properties.notes: _Example: Apply the Fundamental Counting Principle in a context that can be represented by a tree diagram in which there are the same number of branches from each node at each level of the tree._
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Identify differences between applications of combinations and permutations.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Using application-based problems, calculate the number of permutations of a set with _n_ elements. Calculate the number of permutations of _r_ elements taken from a set of _n_ elements.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Using application-based problems, calculate the number of subsets of size _r_ that can be chosen from a set of _n_ elements, explaining this number as the number of combinations "_n_ choose _r_."
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Using application-based problems, calculate the number of combinations with repetitions of _r_ elements from a set of n elements as "(_n_ + _r_ - 1) choose _r_."
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Identify properties of simple graphs, complete graphs, bipartite graphs, complete bipartite graphs, and trees.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Develop optimal solutions of application-based problems using existing and student- created algorithms.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Give an argument for graph properties.
properties.notes: _Example: Explain why a graph has a Euler cycle if and only if the graph is connected and every vertex has even degree. Show that any tree with_ n _vertices has_ n - 1 _edges._
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use shortest path techniques to find optimal shipping routes.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Show that every connected graph has a minimal spanning tree.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use Kruskal's Algorithm and Prim's Algorithm to determine the minimal spanning tree of a weighted graph.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Identify impacts of using a preferential ballot voting system and compare it to single candidate voting and other voting systems.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Analyze the impact of legal and cultural features of political systems on the mathematical aspects of elections.
properties.notes: _Examples: mathematical disadvantages of third parties, the cost of run-off elections_
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Distinguish between weight and power in voting.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use modular arithmetic to apply RSA (Rivest-Shamir-Adleman) public-key cryptosystems.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use matrices and their inverses to encode and decode messages.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Define the constant _e_ in a variety of contexts.
properties.notes: _Example: the total interest earned if a 100% annual rate is continuously compounded._
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Find the conjugate of a complex number; use conjugates to find moduli and quotients of complex numbers.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Represent complex numbers on the complex plane in rectangular and polar form (including real and imaginary numbers), and explain why the rectangular and polar forms of a given complex number represent the same number.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Represent addition, subtraction, multiplication, and conjugation of complex numbers geometrically on the complex plane; use properties of this representation for computation.
properties.notes: _Example:_ $(-1 + \sqrt{3i})^3 = 8$ _because_ $(-1 + \sqrt{3i})$ _has modulus 2 and argument_ $120^{\circ}$.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Calculate the distance between numbers in the complex plane as the modulus of the difference, and the midpoint of a segment as the average of the numbers at its endpoints.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Analyze possible zeros for a polynomial function over the complex numbers by applying the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra, using a graph of the function, or factoring with algebraic identities.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Determine numerically, algebraically, and graphically the limits of functions at specific values and at infinity.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Explain that vector quantities have both magnitude and direction. Represent vector quantities by directed line segments, and use appropriate symbols for vectors and their magnitudes.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Find the components of a vector by subtracting the coordinates of an initial point from the coordinates of a terminal point.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Solve problems involving velocity and other quantities that can be represented by vectors.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Find the scalar (dot) product of two vectors as the sum of the products of corresponding components and explain its relationship to the cosine of the angle formed by two vectors.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Add and subtract vectors.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Multiply a vector by a scalar.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Multiply a vector (regarded as a matrix with one column) by a matrix of suitable dimensions to produce another vector. Work with matrices as transformations of vectors.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Derive the formula for the sum of a finite geometric series (when the common ratio is not 1), and use the formula to solve problems, extending to infinite geometric series.
properties.notes: _Examples: calculate mortgage payments; determine the long-term level of medication if a patient takes 50 mg of a medication every 4 hours, while 70% of the medication is filtered out of the patient's blood._
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Derive and apply the Remainder Theorem: For a polynomial _p(x)_ and a number _a_, the remainder on division by _x - a_ is _p(a)_, so _p(a)_ = 0 if and only if (_x - a_) is a factor of _p(x)._
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Know and apply the Binomial Theorem for the expansion of $(x + y)^n$ in powers of _x_ and _y_ for a positive integer, _n_, where _x_ and _y_ are any numbers.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Rewrite simple rational expressions in different forms; write _a(x)/b(x)_ in the form _q(x) + r(x)/b(x)_, where _a(x), b(x), q(x)_, and _r(x)_ are polynomials with the degree of _r(x)_ less than the degree of _b(x)_, using inspection, long division, or, for the more complicated cases, a computer algebra system.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Add, subtract, multiply, and divide rational expressions.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Explain each step in solving an equation as following from the equality of numbers asserted at the previous step, starting from the assumption that the original equation has a clear-cut solution. Construct a viable argument to justify a solution method. **Include equations that may involve linear, quadratic, polynomial, exponential, logarithmic, absolute value, radical, rational, piecewise, and trigonometric functions, and their inverses.**
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Solve simple rational equations in one variable, and give examples showing how extraneous solutions may arise.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Represent a system of linear equations as a single matrix equation in a vector variable.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Find the inverse of a matrix if it exists and use it to solve systems of linear equations (using technology for matrices of dimension 3 x 3 or greater).
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Compare and contrast families of functions and their representations algebraically, graphically, numerically, and verbally in terms of their key features. _Note: Key features include intercepts; intervals where the function is increasing, decreasing, positive, or negative; maximums and minimums; symmetries (including even and odd); end behavior; asymptotes; and periodicity._ **Families of functions include but are not limited to linear, quadratic, polynomial, exponential, logarithmic, absolute value, radical, rational, piecewise, trigonometric, and their inverses.**
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Calculate and interpret the average rate of change of a function (presented symbolically or as a table) over a specified interval. Estimate the rate of change from a graph. **Extend from polynomial, exponential, logarithmic, and radical to rational and all trigonometric functions.**
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Graph functions expressed symbolically and show key features of the graph, by hand and using technology. Use the equation of functions to identify key features in order to generate a graph.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Compose functions. **Extend to polynomial, trigonometric, radical, and rational functions.**
properties.notes: _Example: If_ T(y) _is the temperature in the atmosphere as a function of height, and_ h(t) _is the height of a weather balloon as a function of time, then_ T(h(t)) _is the temperature at the location of the weather balloon as a function of time._
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Find inverse functions.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use the inverse relationship between exponents and logarithms to solve problems involving logarithms and exponents. **Extend from logarithms with base 2 and 10 to a base of _e_.**
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Identify the effect on the graph of replacing $f(x)$ by $f(x) + k$, $k \cdot f(x)$, $f(k \cdot x)$, and $f(x + k)$ for specific values of $k$ (both positive and negative); find the value of $k$ given the graphs. Extend the analysis to include all trigonometric, rational, and general piecewise-defined functions with and without technology.
properties.notes: _Example: Describe the sequence of transformations that will relate_ y=sin(x) _and_ y=2sin(3x).
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Graph conic sections from second-degree equations, extending from circles and parabolas to ellipses and hyperbolas, using technology to discover patterns.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Solve application-based problems involving parametric and polar equations.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use special triangles to determine geometrically the values of sine, cosine, and tangent for $\frac {\pi}{3}$, $\frac {\pi}{4}$, and $\frac {\pi}{6}$, and use the unit circle to express the values of sine, cosine, and tangent for $\pi - x$, $\pi + x$, and $2\pi - x$ in terms of their values for $x$, where $x$ is any real number.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use the unit circle to explain symmetry (odd and even) and periodicity of trigonometric functions.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Demonstrate that restricting a trigonometric function to a domain on which it is always increasing or always decreasing allows its inverse to be constructed.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use inverse functions to solve trigonometric equations that arise in modeling contexts; evaluate the solutions using technology, and interpret them in terms of the context.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use trigonometric identities to solve problems.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Given the endpoints of the diameter of a circle, use the midpoint formula to find its center and then use the Pythagorean Theorem to find its equation.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Derive the distance formula from the Pythagorean Theorem.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Explain how standard deviation develops from mean absolute deviation.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Calculate the standard deviation for a data set, using technology where appropriate.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Find a linear function for a scatter plot that suggests a linear association and informally assess its fit by plotting and analyzing residuals, including the squares of the residuals, in order to improve its fit.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use technology to find the least-squares line of best fit for two quantitative variables.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use the linear model to solve problems in the context of the given data.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Interpret the slope (rate of change) and the intercept (constant term) of a linear model in the context of the given data.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Give an informal argument for the formulas for the surface area and volume of a sphere, cylinder, pyramid, and cone using dissection arguments, Cavalieri's Principle, and informal limit arguments.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Apply geometric concepts to find missing dimensions to solve surface area or volume problems.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Describe transformations and compositions of transformations as functions that take points in the plane as inputs and give other points as outputs, using informal and formal notation.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Compare transformations which preserve distance and angle measure to those that do not.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Given a geometric figure and a rotation, reflection, or translation, draw the image of the transformed figure using graph paper, tracing paper, or geometry software.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Specify a sequence of rotations, reflections, or translations that will carry a given figure onto another.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Draw figures with different types of symmetries and describe their attributes.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Verify that two triangles are congruent if and only if corresponding pairs of sides and corresponding pairs of angles are congruent.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Verify that two triangles are congruent if (but not only if) the following groups of corresponding parts are congruent: angle-side-angle (ASA), side-angle-side (SAS), side- side-side (SSS), and angle-angle-side (AAS).
properties.notes: _Example: Given two triangles with two pairs of congruent corresponding sides and a pair of congruent included angles, show that there must be a sequence of rigid motions will map one onto the other._
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Verify that a dilation takes a line not passing through the center of the dilation to a parallel line, and leaves a line passing through the center unchanged.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Verify that the dilation of a line segment is longer or shorter in the ratio given by the scale factor.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Verify that two triangles are similar if and only if corresponding pairs of sides are proportional and corresponding pairs of angles are congruent.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Verify that two triangles are similar if (but not only if) two pairs of corresponding angles are congruent (AA), the corresponding sides are proportional (SSS), or two pairs of corresponding sides are proportional and the pair of included angles is congruent (SAS).
properties.notes: _Example: Given two triangles with two pairs of congruent corresponding sides and a pair of congruent included angles, show there must be a set of rigid motions that maps one onto the other._
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Construct figures, using technology and other tools, in order to make and test conjectures about their properties.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Identify different sets of properties necessary to define and construct figures.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Investigate, prove, and apply theorems about lines and angles, including but not limited to: vertical angles are congruent; when a transversal crosses parallel lines, alternate interior angles are congruent and corresponding angles are congruent; the points on the perpendicular bisector of a line segment are those equidistant from the segment's endpoints.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Investigate, prove, and apply theorems about triangles, including but not limited to: the sum of the measures of the interior angles of a triangle is $180^{\circ}$; the base angles of isosceles triangles are congruent; the segment joining the midpoints of two sides of a triangle is parallel to the third side and half the length; a line parallel to one side of a triangle divides the other two proportionally, and conversely; the Pythagorean Theorem using triangle similarity.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Investigate, prove, and apply theorems about parallelograms and other quadrilaterals, including but not limited to both necessary and sufficient conditions for parallelograms and other quadrilaterals, as well as relationships among kinds of quadrilaterals.
properties.notes: _Example: Prove that rectangles are parallelograms with congruent diagonals._
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Derive and apply the constant ratios of the sides in special right triangles ($45^{\circ} -45^{\circ} -90^{\circ}$ and $30^{\circ} -60^{\circ} -90^{\circ}$).
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use similarity to explore and define basic trigonometric ratios, including sine ratio, cosine ratio, and tangent ratio.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Explain and use the relationship between the sine and cosine of complementary angles.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Demonstrate the converse of the Pythagorean Theorem.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use trigonometric ratios and the Pythagorean Theorem to solve right triangles in applied problems, including finding areas of regular polygons.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Factor quadratic expressions with leading coefficients of one, and use the factored form to reveal the zeros of the function it defines.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use the vertex form of a quadratic expression to reveal the maximum or minimum value and the axis of symmetry of the function it defines; complete the square to find the vertex form of quadratics with a leading coefficient of one.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use the properties of exponents to transform expressions for exponential functions.
properties.notes: _Example: Identify percent rate of change in functions such as_ $y = (1.02)^t$, $y = (0.97)^t$, $y = (1.01)^{12t}$, $y = (1.2)^{t/10}$, _and classify them as representing exponential growth or decay._
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use the method of completing the square to transform any quadratic equation in $x$ into an equation of the form $(x - p)^2 = q$ that has the same solutions. Explain how the quadratic formula is derived from this form.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Solve quadratic equations by inspection (such as $x^2 = 49$), taking square roots, completing the square, the quadratic formula, and factoring, as appropriate to the initial form of the equation, and recognize that some solutions may not be real.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Solve a system of two equations in two variables by using linear combinations; contrast situations in which use of linear combinations is more efficient with those in which substitution is more efficient.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Contrast solutions to a system of two linear equations in two variables produced by algebraic methods with graphical and tabular methods.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use function notation, evaluate functions for inputs in their domains, and interpret statements that use function notation in terms of a context. _Note: If f is a function and x is an element of its domain, then_ $f(x)$ _denotes the output of_ $f$ _corresponding to the input_ $x$.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Relate the domain of a function to its graph and, where applicable, to the quantitative relationship it describes. **Limit to linear, quadratic, exponential, and absolute value functions.**
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use arithmetic operations to combine different types of standard functions to write and evaluate functions.
properties.notes: _Example: Given two functions, one representing flow rate of water and the other representing evaporation of that water, combine the two functions to determine the amount of water in a container at a given time._
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use function composition to combine different types of standard functions to write and evaluate functions.
properties.notes: _Example: Given the following relationships, determine what the expression _S(T(t))_ represents._
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Find the approximate solutions of an equation graphically, using tables of values, or finding successive approximations, using technology where appropriate. _Note: Include cases where_ $f(x)$ _is a linear, quadratic, exponential, or absolute value function and_ $g(x)$ _is constant or linear._
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Write explicit and recursive formulas for arithmetic and geometric sequences and connect them to linear and exponential functions.
properties.notes: _Example: A sequence with constant growth will be a linear function, while a sequence with proportional growth will be an exponential function._
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Show that linear functions grow by equal differences over equal intervals, while exponential functions grow by equal factors over equal intervals.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Define linear functions to represent situations in which one quantity changes at a constant rate per unit interval relative to another.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Define exponential functions to represent situations in which a quantity grows or decays by a constant percent rate per unit interval relative to another.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Graph linear and quadratic functions and show intercepts, maxima, and minima.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Graph piecewise-defined functions, including step functions and absolute value functions.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Graph exponential functions, showing intercepts and end behavior.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Summarize categorical data for two categories in two-way frequency tables and represent using segmented bar graphs.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Interpret relative frequencies in the context of categorical data (including joint, marginal, and conditional relative frequencies).
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Identify possible associations and trends in categorical data.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Aggregate data from several groups to find an overall association between two categorical variables.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Recognize and explore situations where the association between two categorical variables is reversed when a third variable is considered (Simpson's Paradox).
properties.notes: _Example: In a certain city, Hospital 1 has a higher fatality rate than Hospital 2. But when considering mildly-injured patients and severely-injured patients as separate groups, Hospital 1 has a lower fatality rate among both groups than Hospital 2, since Hospital 1 is a Level 1 Trauma Center. Thus, Hospital 1 receives most of the severely injured patients who are less likely to survive overall but have a better chance of surviving in Hospital 1 than they would in Hospital 2._
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Find the approximate solutions of an equation graphically, using tables of values, or finding successive approximations, using technology where appropriate. **Extend to cases where $f(x)$ and/or $g(x)$ are polynomial, trigonometric (sine and cosine), logarithmic, radical, and general piecewise functions.**
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Graph polynomial functions expressed symbolically, identifying zeros when suitable factorizations are available, and showing end behavior.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Graph sine and cosine functions expressed symbolically, showing period, midline, and amplitude.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Graph logarithmic functions expressed symbolically, showing intercepts and end behavior.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Graph reciprocal functions expressed symbolically, identifying horizontal and vertical asymptotes.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Graph square root and cube root functions expressed symbolically.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Compare the graphs of inverse functions and the relationships between their key features, including but not limited to quadratic, square root, exponential, and logarithmic functions.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use technology to determine if a given set of data is normal by applying the empirical rule.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Estimate areas under a normal curve to solve problems in context, using calculators, spreadsheets, and tables as appropriate.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Simulate a sampling distribution of sample means from a population with a known distribution, observing the effect of the sample size on the variability.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Demonstrate that the standard deviation of each simulated sampling distribution is the known standard deviation of the population divided by the square root of the sample size.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Verify that a sampling distribution is centered at the population mean and approximately normal if the sample size is large enough.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Verify that 95% of sample means are within two standard deviations of the sampling distribution from the population mean.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Create and interpret a 95% confidence interval based on an observed mean from a sampling distribution.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Explore the behavior of the function $y=e^x$ and its applications.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Explore the behavior of _ln(x)_, the logarithmic function with base _e_, and its applications.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Apply limits of functions at specific values and at infinity in problems involving convergence and divergence.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Add vectors end-to-end, component-wise, and by the parallelogram rule, understanding that the magnitude of a sum of two vectors is not always the sum of the magnitudes.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Given two vectors in magnitude and direction form, determine the magnitude and direction of their sum.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Explain vector subtraction, **_v - w_**, as **_v + (-w)_**, where **_-w_** is the additive inverse of **_w_**, with the same magnitude as **_w_** and pointing in the opposite direction. Represent vector subtraction graphically by connecting the tips in the appropriate order, and perform vector subtraction component-wise.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Represent scalar multiplication graphically by scaling vectors and possibly reversing their direction; perform scalar multiplication component-wise.
properties.notes: _Example:_ $c(v_x, v_y) = (cv_x, cv_y)$
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Compute the magnitude of a scalar multiple _c_**v** using ||_c_**v**|| = |_c_|**v**. Compute the direction of _c_**v** knowing that when |_c_|**v** ≠ 0, the direction of _c_**v** is either along **v** (for _c_ > 0) or against **v** (for _c_ < 0).
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Explain why rational expressions form a system analogous to the rational numbers, which is closed under addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division by a non-zero rational expression.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Find the difference quotient $\frac {f(x+\Delta x) - f(x)}{\Delta x}$ of a function and use it to evaluate the average rate of change at a point.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Explore how the average rate of change of a function over an interval (presented symbolically or as a table) can be used to approximate the instantaneous rate of change at a point as the interval decreases.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Graph rational functions, identifying zeros and asymptotes when suitable factorizations are available, and showing end behavior.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Graph trigonometric functions and their inverses, showing period, midline, amplitude, and phase shift.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Given that a function has an inverse, write an expression for the inverse of the function.
properties.notes: _Example: Given_ $f(x) = 2x^3$ _or_ $f(x) = (x + 1)/(x - 1)$ _for_ x $\neq 1$ _find_ $f^{-1}(x)$.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Verify by composition that one function is the inverse of another.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Read values of an inverse function from a graph or a table, given that the function has an inverse.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Produce an invertible function from a non-invertible function by restricting the domain.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Graph conic sections given their standard form.
properties.notes: _Example: The graph of_ $\frac {x^2}{9} + \frac {(y−3)^2}{4} = 1$ _will be an ellipse centered at (0,3) with major axis 3 and minor axis 2, while the graph of_ $\frac {x^2}{9} − \frac {(y−3)^2}{4} = 1$ _will be a hyperbola centered at (0,3) with asymptotes with slope_ $\neq 2/3.$
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Identify the conic section that will be formed, given its equation in general form.
properties.notes: _Example:_ $5y^2 - 25x^2 = -25$ _will be a hyperbola._
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Graph parametric and polar equations.
properties.author: 1EdTech
